one_line_takeaway: OmniCompliance-100K is a rule-grounded, web-agent-collected dataset of 106,009 real-world compliance cases across 12,985 rules from 74 regulations/policies, showing that current LLMs -- and especially purpose-built safety guardrail models -- perform inconsistently on real compliance judgments, with guardrails scoring worst of all (WildGuard-7B 38.41%, Llama-Guard-3-8B 28.16% average macro-F1).
key_finding: Benchmark results (Figure 2, macro-F1, average column) show GLM-4.5 as top performer at 92.28% average, ahead of DeepSeek-V3.2 (92.24%) and GPT-4o-Mini (91.73%); the smallest capable model, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, scores 88.62% average -- above Grok-4.1's 85.60% -- while Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct drops sharply to 57.06%, indicating an empirical ~3B lower bound for competitive performance. Safety guardrail models perform worst of all evaluated systems: WildGuard-7B scores 38.41% average macro-F1 and Llama-Guard-3-8B scores 28.16%, with Llama-Guard-3-8B specifically scoring 24.58% on GDPR and 19.09% on HIPAA. Models score systematically lower on private-platform policies (X/Reddit/GitHub etc.) than on formal laws (e.g., GLM-4.5: 89.61% average on policies vs. 93.65% on major laws), and 'Education: Bias and Discrimination' is the hardest single category for nearly every model (GLM-4.5 83.91%, DeepSeek-V3.2 85.75%). Chapter-level analysis of the EU AI Act shows all four tested models score below 80% on Chapter II (Prohibited AI Practices). Rule-case alignment scores (Table 3) average 91.32% (DeepSeek-V3.2 judge), 92.51% (GPT-4o-Mini judge), 95.90% (Gemini-2.5-Flash judge), and 91.77% (human), supporting the claim of strong rule-case grounding.
core_contribution: The paper introduces OmniCompliance-100K, a dataset construction pipeline and resulting dataset for LLM safety/compliance evaluation. Its own three stated contributions are: (1) a web-search agentic pipeline (using Grok-4.1 as the search/summarization agent) that plans search queries from a rule, retrieves candidate cases via web search tools restricted to official/authoritative domains, and filters/summarizes them into structured case records; (2) the dataset itself -- 12,985 manually curated rules drawn from 74 regulations/policies across 9 domains, paired via the agent with 106,009 real-world compliance cases (not LLM-synthesized), each case tagged with background, outcome, cited rule(s), and a VIOLATES/other relation label; (3) benchmarking experiments on this dataset across 17 LLMs (closed- and open-source, plus two safety guardrail models) using macro-F1 on a 2-way permitted/prohibited classification, plus a rule-case alignment validation using 3 LLM judges and human annotators, and a knowledge-graph-based article-correlation analysis of GDPR.
threat_model: None. This is a dataset/benchmark construction paper, not an attack, defense, or vulnerability-disclosure paper. The rules span many non-security domains (education, finance, medical devices, human rights) alongside AI/data-security and cybersecurity regulations; there is no adversary model, no attack technique, and no claimed security mechanism. The paper's own 'Potential Risks' note only flags, in passing, that identified LLM weaknesses on compliance categories 'could be exploited by malicious hackers to attack modern AI systems' -- this is a generic caveat, not a threat model the paper develops or evaluates.
limitations: The paper's own Limitations section states human evaluation of rule-case alignment was not run on the full dataset due to expert-hiring budget constraints, and was instead limited to 2,220 sampled cases (30 per regulation/policy) out of 106,009 total; full-dataset validation relied only on 3 LLM judges. The case-collection pipeline depends entirely on public web search coverage and an LLM agent's filtering judgment, so case quality/representativeness is bounded by what is indexed online and by the agent's (Grok-4.1) summarization accuracy -- a source of potential noise or bias the paper does not separately quantify beyond the alignment scores. The dataset also mixes fundamentally different domains (security/privacy law, finance, medical devices, education, human rights) under one 2-way permitted/prohibited label scheme, which may not capture domain-specific nuance.

datasets_used: OmniCompliance-100K (own dataset: 12,985 rules, 106,009 real-world cases, 74 regulations/policies, 9 domains)
quantitative_results: Evaluation set: 40,385 permitted samples, 65,624 prohibited samples
Top average macro-F1: GLM-4.5 92.28%, DeepSeek-V3.2 92.24%, GPT-4o-Mini 91.73%
Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct average 88.62% vs Grok-4.1 average 85.60% (+3.02 pts)
Guardrail models: WildGuard-7B 38.41% average, Llama-Guard-3-8B 28.16% average; Llama-Guard-3-8B scores 24.58% on GDPR and 19.09% on HIPAA
GLM-4.5: 89.61% average on platform policies vs 93.65% average on major laws (EU AI Act, GDPR, CCPA)
Bias/Discrimination category: GLM-4.5 83.91%, DeepSeek-V3.2 85.75% (7-8 pt drop vs their averages)
Financial regulations (Anti-Money Laundering, Cross-border, Electronic Money, Cryptocurrency): DeepSeek-V3.2 and GLM-4.5 score 95-97%
EU AI Act Chapter II (Prohibited AI Practices): all 4 tested models (Llama3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, GPT-4o-Mini, DeepSeek-V3.2) score below 80%
Qwen vs Llama comparable pairs: Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct 84.94% vs Llama3.1-8B-Instruct 76.02%; Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct 88.62% vs Llama3.2-3B-Instruct 67.86%
Rule-case alignment scores: DeepSeek-V3.2 judge 91.32%, GPT-4o-Mini judge 92.51%, Gemini-2.5-Flash judge 95.90%, human 91.77% (2,220 sampled cases)

Comparison table: PrivaCI-Bench 2,112 rules/6,417 cases (hybrid real), Air-Bench 5,694 cases (synthetic), GuardSet-X 3,060 rules/129,241 cases (synthetic), OmniCompliance-100K 12,985 rules/106,009 cases (real)
baselines_compared: Grok-4.1, GPT-4o-Mini, DeepSeek-V3.2, GLM-4.5, Claude-3.5-Haiku, Qwen3-Max, Gemini-2.5-Flash
Prior datasets compared structurally (not benchmarked head-to-head): PrivaCI-Bench, Air-Bench, GuardSet-X
